# Chicago Education Digest
_May 11 – May 25, 2026_

> Covering: K-12 Education & Schools · Board of Education & Governance

## K-12 Education & Schools

Chicago Public Schools is navigating significant financial and governance shifts, alongside a range of student-related challenges. A primary concern is the recently announced $732.5 million budget deficit for the 2026-27 school year, leading to proposals for job cuts and increased class sizes across the district. This deficit is prompting urgent discussions within the Chicago Board of Education, with members calling on state lawmakers for increased funding, including a specific plea to prioritize student needs over potential stadium projects. The financial pressure is already manifesting, with reports of layoffs at specific schools like ChiArts as CPS assumes direct oversight.

Concurrently, the political landscape for the district's leadership is evolving with the upcoming first-ever elected school board. Candidates are beginning to emerge for the Board President position, including a candidate backed by the Chicago Teachers Union, though union members recently voted against raising dues for political campaigns. Beyond governance and budget, several incidents have drawn attention to student well-being and school accountability. These include an alleged teacher attack on a student, the detention of a high school senior and his mother by ICE, warnings issued by police and CPS regarding planned youth gatherings, and calls from lawmakers for accountability following a school play with controversial themes.

## Board of Education & Governance

Chicago Public Schools is navigating a significant financial challenge, with the district announcing a projected $732.5 million budget deficit for the 2026-27 school year. This shortfall has led to district plans for job cuts, increased class sizes, and overall staffing reductions, a situation that has drawn "urgent concerns" from the current Board of Education. Specific impacts are already being felt, as evidenced by teachers and parents at ChiArts raising alarms about potential layoffs following CPS's takeover. In response, Board members have publicly called on state legislators in Springfield to prioritize school funding for students over other large-scale projects.

Concurrently, Chicago is actively preparing for its transition to an elected school board. The petition filing period for candidates has commenced, with individuals beginning to declare their intent to run for the first-ever elected Board of Education seats. A key development in this transition is the emergence of candidates for the elected school board president position, including a Chicago Teachers Union-backed candidate whose entry into the race has reportedly generated discussion and criticism. These developments highlight the ongoing shift in the district's governance structure as the city moves towards a new model of oversight.
